title: Witch Week - Chapter 11 (Fragment 2)
text:
“They can’t go high enough to get over,” said Charles. “Now what?”

That might have been the end of their journey, had not the caretaker’s dog been sniffing about in the kitchen gardens and suddenly caught their scent. It came racing down the long path towards them, yapping. The hoe and the mop took off like startled cats. They soared over the wall, with Charles and Nirupam hanging on anyhow, and went bucking off down the fields beyond. They raced towards the main road, the mop surging, the hoe plunging and trailing, clearing hedges by a whisker and missing trees by inches. They did not slow down until they had put three fields between them and the caretaker’s dog.

“They must hate that dog as much as we do,” Nirupam gasped. “Was it you that did the Simon Says spell?”

“Yes,” said Charles. “Did you do the birds in music?”

“No,” said Nirupam, much to Charles’s surprise. “I did only one thing, and that was secret, but I daren’t stay if the inquisitors are going to bring a witch-detector. They always get you with those.”

“What did you do?” said Charles.

“You know that night all our shoes went into the hall,” said Nirupam. “Well, we had a feast that night. Dan Smith made me get up the floorboards and get the food out. He says I have no right to be so large and so weak,” Nirupam said resentfully, “and I was hating him for it, when I took the boards up and found a pair of running shoes, with spikes, hidden there with the food. I turned those shoes into a chocolate cake. I knew Dan was so greedy that he would eat it all himself. And he did eat it. He didn’t let anyone else have any. You may have noticed that he wasn’t quite himself the next day.”

So much had happened to Charles that particular day, that he could not remember Dan seeming anything at all. He didn’t have the heart to explain all the trouble Nirupam had caused him. “Those were my spikes,” he said sadly. He wobbled along on the mop rather awed at the thought of iron spikes passing through Dan’s stomach. “He must have a digestion like an ostrich!”

“The spikes were turned into cherries,” said Nirupam. “The soles were the cream. The shoes as a whole became what is called a Black Forest gâteau.”

Here they reached the main road and saw the tops of cars whipping past beyond the hedge. “We’ll have to wait for a gap in that traffic,” Charles said. “Stop!” he commanded the mop.

“Stop!” Nirupam cried to the hoe.

Neither implement took the slightest notice. Since Charles and Nirupam did not dare put their feet down for fear of leaving a scent for the dogs, they could find no way of stopping at all. They were carried helplessly over the hedge. Luckily, the road was down in a slight dip, and they had just enough height to clear the whizzing cars. Nirupam frantically bent his huge legs up. Charles tried not to let his legs dangle. Horns honked. He saw faces peering up at them, outraged and grinning. Charles suddenly saw how ridiculous they must look, both in their little blue shorts: himself with the disgraceful dirty old mop head wagging behind him; Nirupam lunging through the air in bunny hops, with a look of anguish on his face.

Horns were still sounding as they cleared the hedge on the other side of the road.

“Oh help!” gasped Nirupam. “Make for the woods, quick, before somebody gets the police!”

Larwood Forest was only a short hillside away and, luckily, their panic seemed to get through to the mop and the hoe. Both put on speed. The wagging and slewing of the mop nearly threw Charles off. The hoe helped itself along by digging its metal end fiercely into the ground, so that Nirupam went upwards like someone on a pogo stick, yelping at each leap. Horns were still sounding from the road as first Nirupam, then Charles reached the trees and plunged in among them. By this time, Nirupam was so far ahead that Charles thought he had lost him. Probably just as well, Charles thought. They might be safer going separate ways. But the mop had other ideas. After dithering a bit, as if it had lost the scent, it set off again at top wallow. Charles was wagged around tree trunks and swayed through prickly undergrowth. Finally he was slewed through a bed of nettles. He yelled. Nirupam yelled too, just beyond the nettles. The hoe tipped him off into a blackberry bush and darted gladly towards an old threadbare broomstick
which was leaning on the other side of the brambles. At the sight, the mop threw Charles into the nettles and went bouncing flirtatiously towards the broomstick too, looking just like a granny on an outing.

vocabulary.definition: Severe mental or physical pain or suffering.
vocabulary.example: Charles suddenly saw how ridiculous they must look, both in their little blue shorts: himself with the disgraceful dirty old mop head wagging behind him; Nirupam lunging through the air in bunny hops, with a look of anguish on his face.

vocabulary.definition: Filled with wonder or admiration.
vocabulary.example: He wobbled along on the mop rather awed at the thought of iron spikes passing through Dan’s stomach.

vocabulary.definition: A prickly scrambling shrub or vine, especially blackberry bushes.
vocabulary.example:
The hoe tipped him off into a blackberry bush and darted gladly towards an old threadbare broomstick
which was leaning on the other side of the brambles.

vocabulary.definition: To leap or jump suddenly.
vocabulary.example: They soared over the wall, with Charles and Nirupam hanging on anyhow, and went bucking off down the fields beyond.

vocabulary.definition: A sweet brown food made from roasted cocoa beans.
vocabulary.example: I turned those shoes into a chocolate cake.

vocabulary.definition: The process by which food is broken down and absorbed in the body.
vocabulary.example: “He must have a digestion like an ostrich!

vocabulary.definition: Shameful and unacceptable.
vocabulary.example: Charles suddenly saw how ridiculous they must look, both in their little blue shorts: himself with the disgraceful dirty old mop head wagging behind him; Nirupam lunging through the air in bunny hops, with a look of anguish on his face.

vocabulary.definition: Being indecisive or uncertain.
vocabulary.example: After dithering a bit, as if it had lost the scent, it set off again at top wallow.

vocabulary.definition: Having an intense and selfish desire for something, especially food or wealth.
vocabulary.example: I knew Dan was so greedy that he would eat it all himself.

vocabulary.definition: A tool, utensil, or other piece of equipment.
vocabulary.example: Neither implement took the slightest notice.

vocabulary.definition: Someone who asks questions aggressively or officially, especially in historical or judicial contexts.
vocabulary.example: “I did only one thing, and that was secret, but I daren’t stay if the inquisitors are going to bring a witch-detector.

vocabulary.definition: A herbaceous plant with stinging hairs.
vocabulary.example: Finally he was slewed through a bed of nettles.

vocabulary.definition: Very angry or shocked.
vocabulary.example: He saw faces peering up at them, outraged and grinning.

vocabulary.definition: A sudden uncontrollable fear or anxiety.
vocabulary.example:
”

Larwood Forest was only a short hillside away and, luckily, their panic seemed to get through to the mop and the hoe.

vocabulary.definition: To move or fall suddenly and quickly downward.
vocabulary.example: They raced towards the main road, the mop surging, the hoe plunging and trailing, clearing hedges by a whisker and missing trees by inches.

vocabulary.definition: With bitterness or indignation at having been treated unfairly.
vocabulary.example: He says I have no right to be so large and so weak,” Nirupam said resentfully, “and I was hating him for it, when I took the boards up and found a pair of running shoes, with spikes, hidden there with the food.

vocabulary.definition: Deserving or inviting mockery or derision.
vocabulary.example: Charles suddenly saw how ridiculous they must look, both in their little blue shorts: himself with the disgraceful dirty old mop head wagging behind him; Nirupam lunging through the air in bunny hops, with a look of anguish on his face.

vocabulary.definition: A distinctive smell, especially one left by a person or animal.
vocabulary.example:
”

That might have been the end of their journey, had not the caretaker’s dog been sniffing about in the kitchen gardens and suddenly caught their scent.

vocabulary.definition: To turn or slide violently or uncontrollably.
vocabulary.example: The wagging and slewing of the mop nearly threw Charles off.

vocabulary.definition: Surprised and alarmed.
vocabulary.example: The hoe and the mop took off like startled cats.

vocabulary.definition: Moving suddenly and powerfully forward or upward.
vocabulary.example: They raced towards the main road, the mop surging, the hoe plunging and trailing, clearing hedges by a whisker and missing trees by inches.

vocabulary.definition: To follow behind or drag along.
vocabulary.example: They raced towards the main road, the mop surging, the hoe plunging and trailing, clearing hedges by a whisker and missing trees by inches.

vocabulary.definition: A dense growth of shrubs and small trees, especially under taller trees.
vocabulary.example: Charles was wagged around tree trunks and swayed through prickly undergrowth.

vocabulary.definition: To move or roll about with difficulty or in an indulgent way.
vocabulary.example: After dithering a bit, as if it had lost the scent, it set off again at top wallow.

vocabulary.definition: A very narrow margin or small distance.
vocabulary.example: They raced towards the main road, the mop surging, the hoe plunging and trailing, clearing hedges by a whisker and missing trees by inches.

vocabulary.definition: A device or person that detects witches or witchcraft.
vocabulary.example: “I did only one thing, and that was secret, but I daren’t stay if the inquisitors are going to bring a witch-detector.

vocabulary.definition: To bark sharply or persistently.
vocabulary.example: It came racing down the long path towards them, yapping.
